title: Question Words
description: Ask anything — kya, kab, kahan, kaise, kyun, kaun, kitna.
culturalNote: In Delhi Hindi, question words often go BEFORE the verb but AFTER the subject — 'tum kahan ja rahi ho?' (you where are you going?). But in rapid casual speech, word order is flexible. The tone also helps — rising intonation turns any statement into a yes/no question without adding 'kya'.

exercises.note: 'Kya' has two jobs: 1) 'What?' — 'kya hua?' = what happened? 'kya chahiye?' = what do you want? 2) Yes/no question marker at start of sentence: 'Kya tum aaogi?' = will you come? In fast Delhi speech, 'kya' at end also works: 'Tum aaogi kya?'

'Kab' = when. 'Kab aauungi tum?' = when will you come? 'Kab se?' = since when? 'Kab tak?' = until when? / how long? 'Kab hua yeh?' = when did this happen? 'Tab se kab tak?' = from then until when? A very versatile question word.

'Kahan' = where. 'Kahan ja rahi ho?' = where are you going? 'Kahan hai woh?' = where is she? 'Kahan se?' = from where? 'Kahan tak?' = up to where? / how far? In Delhi: 'Kahan ka hoon?' = where are you from? (common opener).

exercises.prompt: You want to ask 'What happened?' after hearing bad news. What do you say?

exercises.note: 'Kaise' = how. 'Kaise ho?' = how are you? 'Kaise kiya?' = how did you do it? 'Yeh kaise possible hai?' = how is this possible? 'Kaise chalega?' = how will this work? Also used to express disbelief: 'Yeh kaise ho sakta hai?' = how can this be?

'Kyun' = why. 'Kyun aayi nahi?' = why didn't you come? 'Kyun nahi?' = why not? (agreement or disbelief). 'Toh kyun?' = so why? 'Kyun bhai?' = why man? 'Kyun' at the end sounds more casual: 'Aayi nahi kyun?' = why didn't you come?

exercises.sentence: _____ ja rahi ho? Office ya mall?
exercises.sentenceEnglish: Where are you going? Office or mall?

exercises.note: 'Kaun' = who. 'Yeh kaun hai?' = who is this? 'Kaun aayi?' = who came? 'Kaun sa?' = which one? 'Kaun sa day?' = which day? Note: 'Kaun' vs 'Kaun sa' — 'kaun' = who (person), 'kaun sa' = which (choosing one). Both very common.

'Kitna' (m): 'kitna time hai?' = how much time is there? 'Yeh kitna hai?' = how much is this? 'Kitni' (f): 'kitni door hai?' = how far is it? 'Kitni baar?' = how many times? 'Kitne' (pl): 'kitne log?' = how many people? Essential for shopping and directions.

exercises.prompt: You want to ask a shopkeeper 'How much is this?' What do you say?

exercises.english: How / What kind of (about a thing)
exercises.note: 'Kaisa' (m): 'khana kaisa tha?' = how was the food? 'Kaisi' (f): 'movie kaisi thi?' = how was the movie? 'Tum kaisi ho?' = how are you? (to a woman). 'Yeh idea kaisa hai?' = how's this idea? Different from 'kaise' (how, for actions) — 'kaisa/kaisi' describes quality.

exercises.sentenceEnglish: You didn't come to the party, why?

exercises.instruction: Match the question word to its English

exercises.prompt: Your friend asks about a movie you watched. She asks how it was. What does she say?
exercises.hint: 'Movie' is feminine → kaisi (how was it — describing quality)
